facts.keyInsight: LangGraph uses directed acyclic graphs to model agent workflows, enabling explicit state transitions, parallel branching, and conditional routing. Each node is a processing step, edges define transitions. Supports checkpointing and human-in-the-loop interrupts.
facts.relevance: LIAN batch-launch wave planning uses a similar DAG concept for conflict-group serialization. Could adopt explicit state machine transitions for worker lifecycle (planned->running->completed/failed) instead of status field patching.

facts.keyInsight: AutoGen enables multi-agent collaboration through structured conversations. Agents negotiate task allocation, share context through message history, and can request human feedback at decision points. Supports nested conversations for subtask decomposition.
facts.relevance: LIAN workers currently operate in isolation with no inter-worker communication. Adding a lightweight message bus for conflict resolution and context sharing could reduce duplicate work and improve coordination.

facts.keyInsight: Claude Code hooks allow pre/post execution guards on tool calls. Can enforce file-scope restrictions, validate outputs before commit, and trigger side effects (notifications, audits) on specific events.
facts.relevance: LIAN seed-constitution rules are enforced at the task-contract level but not at the tool-call level. Adding Claude Code hooks for forbiddenFiles enforcement would provide defense-in-depth against constitution violations.

facts.keyInsight: Reflexion agents improve by reflecting on failures: they generate self-critiques after task failures, store them in episodic memory, and reference them in subsequent attempts. This creates a feedback loop where the agent learns from its own mistakes without weight updates.
facts.relevance: LIAN classify-self-cycle-failure.js classifies failures but does not generate actionable self-critiques for future cycles. Adding a reflection step that stores failure analysis in meta-signals would enable the system to avoid repeating the same mistakes.
facts.source: Shinn et al. 2023 - Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

facts.keyInsight: CrewAI assigns agents specific roles (researcher, writer, reviewer) with explicit delegation chains. A manager agent can delegate subtasks to specialized agents, each with their own tools and constraints. Supports sequential, parallel, and hierarchical execution modes.
facts.relevance: LIAN workers have role packets but no delegation chain. A worker cannot spawn a sub-worker for a specialized subtask. Adding bounded sub-worker delegation (with the same conflict-group and file-scope constraints) could improve task decomposition quality.

facts.keyInsight: Swarm uses function calling to route tasks between specialized agents. Each agent exposes handoff functions that transfer context to another agent. No central orchestrator — agents self-route based on the current context and their capabilities.
facts.relevance: LIAN Command Steward acts as a central orchestrator. A hybrid approach where workers can hand off subtasks to other workers (within file-scope bounds) could reduce Command Steward bottleneck while maintaining safety through the existing conflict-group system.

facts.keyInsight: MemGPT implements a tiered memory system for agents: in-context memory (working), archival memory (long-term), and recall memory (episodic). The agent actively manages its own memory, deciding what to store, retrieve, and forget. This enables coherent behavior over extended interactions.
facts.relevance: LIAN meta-signals.json and fact-events.ndjson provide raw memory but no retrieval or prioritization layer. Adding a structured memory system that ranks facts by relevance to the current task could improve decision quality in issue production and worker dispatch.
facts.source: Packer et al. 2024 - MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
